title: Kougelhopf
subtitle: Alsatian raisin and almond brioche in a fluted mould
excerpt: Kougelhopf is the enriched yeast cake of Alsace: a tall, fluted ring of brioche-like dough studded with rum-soaked raisins and topped with whole almonds pressed into the grooves of the mould. It is lightly sweet, moist, and rich with butter. The characteristic high, domed shape comes entirely from the traditional kougelhopf mould; a deep fluted ring that the dough climbs during proving. Serve dusted with icing sugar, sliced at the table.
sourceNotes: Kougelhopf (also spelled Gugelhupf, Gugelhopf, or kougelhouf) is associated with Alsace and the wider Upper Rhineland region straddling France, Germany, and Austria. The distinctive mould has been made in terracotta in the Souflenheim region of Alsace since at least the eighteenth century. The cake appears in recipes by Marie-Antoine Carême and later in Escoffier. It is a specialty cake associated with Sunday mornings and the Christmas and Easter seasons.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely before freezing. Thaw at room temperature for several hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be retarded in the fridge after the first rise for up to 12 hours. Transfer to the mould and prove at room temperature for 60 to 90 minutes before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yeasted dough that contains butter, eggs, sugar, or milk in addition to flour, water, and salt. Enrichments add flavour and softness but inhibit gluten development and slow the yeast, requiring longer kneading and fermentation. Classic enriched doughs include brioche, kougelhopf, baba dough, and panettone. As a rule: the more butter and eggs, the longer the dough needs to develop. Cold enriched doughs become more manageable after refrigeration, as the butter solidifies and makes the dough firmer.

content.text: (heavily buttered and egg-rich. Butter goes in last, after the gluten is established, and the dough is beaten rather than kneaded at the end to distribute it. The mould must be very generously buttered and the almonds pressed into the grooves before the dough goes in) they will become the top when the cake is turned out. The kougelhopf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped and the top is well browned.
Soak the raisins and make the dough

Soak 120 g raisins in 2 tbsp rum (or warm water) for 30 minutes. Drain. Mix 450 g flour, 7 g yeast, 60 g sugar, and 6 g salt. Add 150 ml warm milk and 3 beaten eggs. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Add 150 g softened butter in small pieces, beating vigorously between each addition; use a stand mixer with a dough hook if you have one. Beat for a further 4 minutes until the dough is glossy and stretchy. Mix in the drained raisins. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until noticeably puffed.
Prepare the mould, shape, and prove

Butter the kougelhopf mould generously with 30 g softened butter, making sure every flute is coated. Press a blanched almond into each groove of the mould. Knock back the dough and form into a ring shape; poke a hole through the centre with floured fingers and lower into the mould, pressing gently to fill evenly. The mould should be about half full. Cover and prove for 50 to 70 minutes until the dough reaches the rim of the mould.

Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until deep golden and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. Turn out onto a rack after 5 minutes; if it sticks, wait another 5 minutes. Cool completely before dusting with icing sugar.

items.problem: Kougelhopf stuck to the mould and came out in pieces
items.solution: The mould was not buttered thoroughly, or the cake was turned out before it had cooled slightly and set its shape. Butter every crevice of the mould and let the cake rest in the mould for at least 5 minutes before inverting.

items.problem: Top is very dark before the centre is cooked
items.solution: Cover loosely with foil after 25 minutes and continue baking. The tall shape means the top colours faster than the inside cooks through.

items.problem: Raisins sank to the bottom of the mould
items.solution: This is common with high-hydration enriched doughs. Toss the drained raisins in a teaspoon of flour before adding to the dough — it helps them stay suspended.
